title: Container root does not have write permissions on /sys/fs/cgroup directory
body:
### Issue Description

Podman doesn't seem to create an isolated `/sys/fs/cgroup` directory inside the rootless containers and even the container root has only read permission on it. Is there a way to work around this with `cgroups v1`? I only want the container root to able to write to the `/sys/fs/cgroup` since my process (running inside the container as `root`) uses `cgroup` isolation.
Note that its okay even if the `cgroup` isolation doesn't work inside the containers but I at least want my process to be able to run without errors. (basically I want some way to mimic the `cgroup` structure inside the container so that my process does not break).

I also tried using podman's `--cgroupns=private`, but even that didn't work.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue:
1. `podman run  --rm -ti fedora`
2. `ls -la /sys/fs/cgroup/` (inside the container - see that the root does not have write permission on the some folders like `memory`)


### Describe the results you received

I see the root inside the container does not have write permission on directories like `/sys/fs/cgroup/memory`, etc not allowing new cgroup directories to be created inside the container.
```
[root@22917e4570bf /]# ls -la /sys/fs/cgroup/
total 0
drwxrwxrwt 13 root   root   340 May 12 12:51 .
drwxr-xr-x  8 nobody nobody   0 May 12 08:50 ..
drwxr-xr-x  2 nobody nobody   0 May  6 01:43 blkio
lrwxrwxrwx  1 root   root    11 May 12 12:51 cpu -> cpu,cpuacct
drwxr-xr-x  2 nobody nobody   0 May  6 01:43 cpu,cpuacct
lrwxrwxrwx  1 root   root    11 May 12 12:51 cpuacct -> cpu,cpuacct
drwxr-xr-x  2 nobody nobody   0 May  6 01:43 cpuset
drwxr-xr-x  2 nobody nobody   0 May  6 01:43 devices
drwxr-xr-x  3 nobody nobody   0 May  6 01:43 freezer
drwxr-xr-x  2 nobody nobody   0 May  6 01:43 hugetlb
drwxr-xr-x  2 nobody nobody   0 May  6 01:43 memory
lrwxrwxrwx  1 root   root    16 May 12 12:51 net_cls -> net_cls,net_prio
drwxr-xr-x  2 nobody nobody   0 May  6 01:43 net_cls,net_prio
lrwxrwxrwx  1 root   root    16 May 12 12:51 net_prio -> net_cls,net_prio
drwxr-xr-x  2 nobody nobody   0 May  6 01:43 perf_event
drwxr-xr-x  2 nobody nobody   0 May  6 01:43 pids
drwxr-xr-x  2 nobody nobody   0 May  9 06:56 systemd
[root@22917e4570bf /]#
```

### Describe the results you expected

I expect the container root to have `rw` permission on the all the files and folders inside the `/sys/fs/cgroup` directory.

### podman info output

```yaml
If you are unable to run podman info for any reason, please provide the podman version, operating system and its version and the architecture you are running.
podman version - 4.4.2
OS Info:

$ cat /etc/os-release
NAME="Red Hat Enterprise Linux Server"
VERSION="7.9 (Maipo)"
ID="rhel"
ID_LIKE="fedora"
VARIANT="Server"

ANSI_COLOR="0;31"
CPE_NAME="cpe:/o:redhat:enterprise_linux:7.9:GA:server"
HOME_URL="https://www.redhat.com/"
BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugzilla.redhat.com/"

REDHAT_BUGZILLA_PRODUCT="Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7"
REDHAT_BUGZILLA_PRODUCT_VERSION=7.9
REDHAT_SUPPORT_PRODUCT="Red Hat Enterprise Linux"
REDHAT_SUPPORT_PRODUCT_VERSION="7.9"
$
```
```


### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

NA

### Additional information

NA

comments.body: cgroup v1 is not supported in rootless mode.

@rhatdan Thanks for the confirmation. Is there any known workaround (need not be very clean) using other [--cgroupns](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-run.1.html#cgroupns-mode) options like `cgroupns=<namespace>`? Some options I can think of is:
1. `cgroupns=<path_of_new_namespace>`
2. create a sub cgroup (ex: `/sys/fs/cgroup/cpu/my-group`) and give the non-root user WRITE access to it and then providing that path (`/sys/fs/cgroup/my-group`) using [--cgroup-parent](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-run.1.html#cgroup-parent-path)
3. Create a folder structure similar to `/sys/fs/cgroup/` locally and bind mount it to `/sys/fs/cgroup` inside the container.

Do you think any of the above options might work? (Since cgroupv2 is not feasible to me at the moment)

_[I am assuming these options are supported with cgroups v1 since its not mentioned in the documentation that they aren't. Please do correct me if I am wrong though.]_
I know of no way to get around this.  @giuseppe thoughts?
You could chown the files first then bind mount the cgroup structure inside the container.  Be careful though, cgroup delegation is not safe with cgroup v1
closing the issue since there was no further feedback
Thanks @giuseppe for the suggestion. I will try this out.
